expanded_description: Substitution reactions of alkanes involve the replacement of hydrogen atoms with other atoms or groups, most commonly through free radical halogenation. This section explores the mechanism, selectivity, and practical applications of alkane substitution reactions, which are fundamental to understanding organic reactivity and industrial chemical production.

core_concept.principle: Free Radical Halogenation Proceeds Through Chain Reaction Mechanism
core_concept.explanation: Alkanes undergo substitution reactions with halogens ($\text{Cl}_2$, $\text{Br}_2$) through a free radical chain mechanism involving three stages: initiation (radical formation), propagation (chain reaction), and termination (radical combination). The reaction requires heat or UV light to break the halogen-halogen bond homolytically, generating reactive halogen radicals that abstract hydrogen atoms from the alkane, creating alkyl radicals that then react with halogen molecules to propagate the chain.

image.alt: Diagram showing free radical halogenation mechanism with initiation, propagation, and termination steps
image.caption: Free radical halogenation: three-stage chain reaction mechanism (initiation → propagation → termination).

sections.section_title: What Are Substitution Reactions of Alkanes?
sections.introduction: Alkanes are generally unreactive due to their strong $\text{C}-\text{C}$ and $\text{C}-\text{H}$ $\sigma$-bonds and lack of functional groups. However, under appropriate conditions (heat or UV light), alkanes undergo substitution reactions where hydrogen atoms are replaced by halogen atoms. This free radical halogenation is the most important reaction of alkanes and serves as the foundation for understanding radical chemistry in organic synthesis and industrial processes.

key_concept.definition: A substitution reaction of alkanes is a chemical reaction in which one or more hydrogen atoms in an alkane are replaced by halogen atoms (Cl, Br) through a free radical chain mechanism, producing haloalkanes and hydrogen halide as products.
key_concept.context: Free radical halogenation is important industrially for producing chlorinated solvents (chloroform, carbon tetrachloride), refrigerants, and polymer precursors. The reaction shows selectivity based on radical stability: tertiary > secondary > primary hydrogens.

image.caption: General reaction: $\text{R}-\text{H} + \text{X}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{heat/UV}} \text{R}-\text{X} + \text{HX}$ where X = Cl or Br.

principles.statement: Free radical halogenation proceeds through a chain reaction mechanism with three distinct stages: initiation (radical generation), propagation (chain carrying steps), and termination (radical combination).
principles.application: This mechanism explains why small amounts of initiator (heat/light) can convert large quantities of reactants: each initiation event generates radicals that propagate through many cycles before termination. Understanding this mechanism allows control of reaction conditions and product distribution.
principles.analogy: Think of the chain reaction like a line of dominoes: initiation is pushing the first domino (requires energy input), propagation is the dominoes falling (self-sustaining), and termination is when the dominoes stop falling (radicals combine). One push can knock down many dominoes.
principles.example: Methane chlorination: Initiation: $\text{Cl}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{UV}} 2\text{Cl}^\bullet$. Propagation: $\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{HCl} + \text{CH}_3^\bullet$, then $\text{CH}_3^\bullet + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}^\bullet$. One $\text{Cl}^\bullet$ can convert thousands of $\text{CH}_4$ molecules before termination.

principles.statement: The selectivity of halogenation depends on the relative stability of alkyl radicals formed: tertiary ($3^\circ$) > secondary ($2^\circ$) > primary ($1^\circ$) > methyl, due to hyperconjugation and inductive effects.
principles.application: This stability order predicts which hydrogen atoms are preferentially abstracted. Bromination is more selective than chlorination because bromine radicals are less reactive and more sensitive to radical stability differences. This principle guides synthesis planning for selective functionalization.
principles.analogy: Radical stability is like a comfort level: tertiary radicals are most 'comfortable' (stable) because they have more carbon 'friends' to share electron density with through hyperconjugation. Primary radicals are least 'comfortable' (unstable) with fewer carbon neighbors.
principles.example: Propane chlorination: secondary hydrogens are abstracted 4× faster than primary hydrogens at $25^\circ\text{C}$. Product ratio: 2-chloropropane (57%) > 1-chloropropane (43%), despite having fewer secondary hydrogens (2 vs. 6 primary).

components.definition: Free radical halogenation involves homolytic bond cleavage (each atom gets one electron), generating radicals with unpaired electrons that are highly reactive intermediates.
components.interpretation: The reaction requires energy input (heat $\Delta$ or light $h\nu$) to initiate radical formation. Chlorination is faster but less selective; bromination is slower but more selective. Fluorination is too violent; iodination is too slow (unfavorable thermodynamics).

image.alt: Diagram showing alkyl radical structure with unpaired electron
image.caption: Alkyl radical: carbon with unpaired electron ($sp^2$ hybridized, planar geometry).

sections.introduction: Understanding the detailed mechanism of free radical halogenation is essential for predicting reaction outcomes, controlling selectivity, and troubleshooting industrial processes. The mechanism consists of three distinct stages, each with characteristic steps and energy requirements.

process_flow.description: Homolytic cleavage of halogen-halogen bond generates two halogen radicals
process_flow.mechanism: Heat or UV light provides energy to break the $\text{X}-\text{X}$ bond homolytically (each atom gets one electron). This is the only step that requires external energy input.
process_flow.result: Two reactive halogen radicals ($\text{X}^\bullet$) are formed, which initiate the chain reaction

process_flow.description: Halogen radical abstracts hydrogen from alkane, forming alkyl radical and hydrogen halide
process_flow.mechanism: The halogen radical attacks a $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond, abstracting hydrogen atom (with one electron) to form $\text{HX}$ and leaving an alkyl radical ($\text{R}^\bullet$). This is the rate-determining step.
process_flow.result: Alkyl radical formed; hydrogen halide ($\text{HX}$) produced as byproduct
applications: Determines selectivity based on radical stability

process_flow.description: Alkyl radical reacts with halogen molecule, forming haloalkane and regenerating halogen radical
process_flow.mechanism: The alkyl radical attacks a halogen molecule, abstracting one halogen atom to form the haloalkane product and regenerating a halogen radical to continue the chain.
process_flow.result: Haloalkane product formed; halogen radical regenerated to continue chain

process_flow.description: Two radicals combine to form stable molecule, ending the chain
process_flow.mechanism: Any two radicals in the reaction mixture can combine: $\text{X}^\bullet + \text{X}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{X}_2$, $\text{R}^\bullet + \text{X}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{R}-\text{X}$, or $\text{R}^\bullet + \text{R}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{R}-\text{R}$. These steps remove radicals from the system.
process_flow.result: Chain reaction stops; various byproducts may form (alkane dimers, excess halogen)
applications: Can be minimized by controlling radical concentration

worked_examples.title: Writing the Complete Mechanism for Methane Chlorination
worked_examples.problem: Write all steps (initiation, propagation, termination) for the chlorination of methane to form chloromethane.

step_by_step.explanation: Write initiation step: homolytic cleavage of $\text{Cl}-\text{Cl}$ bond.
Write propagation step 1: chlorine radical abstracts hydrogen from methane.
Write propagation step 2: methyl radical reacts with chlorine molecule.
Write termination steps: any two radicals combine.

worked_examples.final_answer: Complete mechanism: Initiation ($\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Cl}^\bullet$), Propagation 1 ($\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{HCl} + \text{CH}_3^\bullet$), Propagation 2 ($\text{CH}_3^\bullet + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}^\bullet$), Termination (various radical combinations). Net reaction: $\text{CH}_4 + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{HCl}$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Free radical chain mechanism: initiation requires energy input; propagation is self-sustaining; termination stops the chain. One initiation event can produce thousands of product molecules.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Product Distribution for Propane Chlorination
worked_examples.problem: Propane has 6 primary hydrogens and 2 secondary hydrogens. Given that secondary hydrogens are abstracted 4× faster than primary at $25^\circ\text{C}$, calculate the expected product ratio of 2-chloropropane to 1-chloropropane.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the number of each type of hydrogen and relative reactivity.

worked_examples.final_answer: Expected product ratio: 57% 2-chloropropane (secondary) : 43% 1-chloropropane (primary), or approximately 1.3:1. Despite having fewer secondary hydrogens (2 vs. 6), the higher reactivity of secondary hydrogens leads to more secondary product.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Product distribution depends on both number of hydrogens AND relative reactivity: $\text{Product} \propto (\text{number of H}) \times (\text{relative reactivity})$. Secondary hydrogens are more reactive due to more stable secondary radical intermediate.

sections.introduction: Not all hydrogen atoms in an alkane are equally reactive toward halogenation. The selectivity depends on the stability of the alkyl radical intermediate formed, which varies with the degree of substitution. Understanding selectivity is crucial for predicting major products and designing synthetic routes.

categories.description: Alkyl radicals follow the stability order: tertiary > secondary > primary > methyl, due to hyperconjugation and inductive effects
categories.detailed_mechanism: Radical stability increases with the number of alkyl groups attached to the radical carbon. Alkyl groups donate electron density through hyperconjugation (overlap of $\text{C}-\text{H}$ $\sigma$-bonds with the half-filled $p$ orbital) and inductive effects, stabilizing the electron-deficient radical center.

examples.explanation: Tertiary radical ($3^\circ$): three alkyl groups, most stable. Secondary radical ($2^\circ$): two alkyl groups, moderately stable. Primary radical ($1^\circ$): one alkyl group, least stable. Methyl radical: no alkyl groups, very unstable.

image.alt: Diagram comparing primary, secondary, and tertiary radical stability
image.caption: Radical stability: $3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ > \text{methyl}$ due to hyperconjugation.

examples.explanation: Each adjacent $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond can overlap with the radical $p$ orbital, delocalizing the unpaired electron. Tertiary radicals have 9 hyperconjugative interactions; secondary have 6; primary have 3; methyl has 0.

categories.description: Bromination is much more selective than chlorination due to differences in radical reactivity and transition state energy
categories.detailed_mechanism: Chlorine radicals are more reactive (less selective) because the $\text{H}-\text{Cl}$ bond formed is stronger, making the hydrogen abstraction step more exothermic. Bromine radicals are less reactive (more selective) because the $\text{H}-\text{Br}$ bond is weaker, making hydrogen abstraction endothermic and more sensitive to radical stability differences.

examples.explanation: Propane chlorination at $25^\circ\text{C}$: 57% 2-chloropropane, 43% 1-chloropropane (selectivity ratio 4:1). Propane bromination at $25^\circ\text{C}$: 97% 2-bromopropane, 3% 1-bromopropane (selectivity ratio 97:1). Bromination is 24× more selective.

image.alt: Comparison of chlorination and bromination selectivity
image.caption: Bromination is much more selective than chlorination due to reactivity-selectivity principle.

examples.explanation: More reactive radicals (Cl$^\bullet$) are less selective; less reactive radicals (Br$^\bullet$) are more selective. This is because highly reactive species react quickly with any available hydrogen, while less reactive species wait for the most favorable (most stable radical-forming) position.

categories.description: Free radical halogenation often produces mixtures of mono-, di-, tri-, and tetra-substituted products
categories.detailed_mechanism: Once a haloalkane is formed, it can undergo further halogenation because the remaining $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds are still reactive. This leads to product mixtures that are difficult to separate. Using excess alkane minimizes multiple substitution.

examples.explanation: Methane + excess $\text{Cl}_2$ produces mixture: $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$ (chloromethane), $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$ (dichloromethane), $\text{CHCl}_3$ (chloroform), $\text{CCl}_4$ (carbon tetrachloride). Industrial separation required.

Use large excess of alkane relative to halogen (e.g., 10:1 $\text{CH}_4:\text{Cl}_2$). This increases probability that $\text{Cl}^\bullet$ encounters unreacted $\text{CH}_4$ rather than already-halogenated product.

sections.key_concept: Selectivity depends on radical stability ($3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ$) and halogen type (Br more selective than Cl). Bromination gives predominantly the most substituted product; chlorination gives mixtures. Multiple substitution is a common problem minimized by excess alkane.

sections.introduction: Understanding the energy changes during free radical halogenation explains why the reaction requires initiation energy, why propagation is self-sustaining, and why chlorination is faster but less selective than bromination. Energy diagrams illustrate the thermodynamic and kinetic factors controlling the reaction.

principles.principle: Bond Dissociation Energies Determine Reaction Energetics
principles.statement: The energetics of each step depend on bond dissociation energies (BDE): weaker bonds are easier to break; stronger bonds formed release more energy.
principles.application: Initiation requires energy to break $\text{X}-\text{X}$ bond ($\text{Cl}-\text{Cl}$: $242 \text{ kJ/mol}$; $\text{Br}-\text{Br}$: $193 \text{ kJ/mol}$). Propagation step 1 (H abstraction) is endothermic for Br but exothermic for Cl, explaining selectivity differences. Overall reaction is exothermic for both Cl and Br.
principles.analogy: Think of bond energies like bank accounts: breaking bonds withdraws energy (costs money); forming bonds deposits energy (earns money). The reaction proceeds if total deposits exceed total withdrawals (net exothermic).
principles.example: Methane chlorination: Initiation ($\text{Cl}-\text{Cl}$ break: $+242 \text{ kJ/mol}$), Propagation 1 ($\text{C}-\text{H}$ break $+439$, $\text{H}-\text{Cl}$ form $-431$: net $+8 \text{ kJ/mol}$), Propagation 2 ($\text{Cl}-\text{Cl}$ break $+242$, $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ form $-351$: net $-109 \text{ kJ/mol}$). Overall: exothermic.

principles.statement: For exothermic reactions (chlorination), the transition state resembles reactants (early TS); for endothermic reactions (bromination), the transition state resembles products (late TS).
principles.application: In bromination, the transition state has significant radical character, so radical stability strongly affects the activation energy. In chlorination, the transition state has little radical character, so radical stability has less effect. This explains why bromination is more selective.
principles.analogy: Think of the transition state as a point on a journey: for exothermic reactions (going downhill), the transition state is near the start (reactants); for endothermic reactions (going uphill), the transition state is near the end (products). The closer to products, the more product stability matters.
principles.example: Chlorination: early transition state, radical stability has small effect on $E_a$ (low selectivity). Bromination: late transition state, radical stability has large effect on $E_a$ (high selectivity).

worked_examples.title: Calculating Overall Enthalpy Change for Methane Chlorination
worked_examples.problem: Calculate $\Delta H^\circ$ for the overall reaction $\text{CH}_4 + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{HCl}$ using bond dissociation energies: $\text{C}-\text{H} = 439 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $\text{Cl}-\text{Cl} = 242 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $\text{C}-\text{Cl} = 351 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $\text{H}-\text{Cl} = 431 \text{ kJ/mol}$.
worked_examples.final_answer: $\Delta H^\circ = -101 \text{ kJ/mol}$. The reaction is exothermic (releases energy), which explains why it is thermodynamically favorable once initiated. The negative enthalpy drives the reaction forward after the initial energy input for initiation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Enthalpy calculation: $\Delta H^\circ = \sum(\text{BDE}_{\text{broken}}) - \sum(\text{BDE}_{\text{formed}})$. Negative $\Delta H^\circ$ indicates exothermic reaction; positive indicates endothermic.

sections.introduction: Free radical halogenation of alkanes has significant industrial importance for producing chlorinated solvents, refrigerants, and polymer precursors. However, environmental and safety concerns have led to restrictions on some products. Understanding both applications and hazards is essential for responsible chemistry practice.

components.function: Chemical intermediate; silicone production; methylating agent
components.key_feature: Produced by methane chlorination; gas at room temperature ($\text{BP} = -24^\circ\text{C}$); toxic; used to make silicones and tetramethyllead (historically)

components.function: Solvent for paint stripping; pharmaceutical manufacturing; aerosol propellant
components.key_feature: Liquid ($\text{BP} = 40^\circ\text{C}$); good solvent for organic compounds; suspected carcinogen; being phased out in many applications

components.function: Historical anesthetic; solvent; precursor to Teflon (via chlorodifluoromethane)
components.key_feature: Liquid ($\text{BP} = 61^\circ\text{C}$); toxic; forms phosgene ($\text{COCl}_2$) on exposure to air/light; stored with ethanol stabilizer

components.function: Historical solvent and fire extinguisher; now restricted
components.key_feature: Liquid ($\text{BP} = 77^\circ\text{C}$); highly toxic (liver damage); ozone-depleting; production banned in many countries under Montreal Protocol

components.function: Monomer for PVC production (pipes, flooring, medical devices)
components.key_feature: Produced from ethylene via chlorination; carcinogenic; polymerized to make PVC, one of the most widely used plastics

components.function: Regulation and replacement of hazardous compounds
components.key_feature: Ozone depletion (CFCs); toxicity (liver, kidney damage); carcinogenicity; persistence in environment; green chemistry alternatives being developed

sections.section_title: Common Mistakes in Understanding Alkane Substitution
sections.introduction: Students often make predictable errors when learning free radical halogenation. Understanding these pitfalls helps ensure accurate mechanistic understanding and correct prediction of reaction outcomes.

categories.type: Mistake: Confusing Homolytic and Heterolytic Cleavage
categories.description: Assuming bonds break heterolytically (ionic) instead of homolytically (radical)
categories.detailed_mechanism: Free radical reactions involve homolytic cleavage (each atom gets one electron, forming radicals). Students sometimes draw heterolytic cleavage (one atom gets both electrons, forming ions), which is incorrect for radical mechanisms.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: $\text{Cl}-\text{Cl} \rightarrow \text{Cl}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$ (heterolytic, ionic). CORRECT: $\text{Cl}-\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{h\nu} 2\text{Cl}^\bullet$ (homolytic, radical). Radical mechanisms use fishhook arrows (single electron); ionic mechanisms use full arrows (electron pair).

categories.description: Expecting halogenation to occur only at the most substituted position
categories.detailed_mechanism: While secondary and tertiary hydrogens are more reactive, primary hydrogens still react to some extent. Students often predict only the most substituted product, ignoring that mixtures are always formed (especially with chlorination).

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 'Propane + $\text{Cl}_2$ gives only 2-chloropropane.' CORRECT: Propane + $\text{Cl}_2$ gives 57% 2-chloropropane + 43% 1-chloropropane at $25^\circ\text{C}$. Both products form; selectivity is not 100%.
examples.equation: $\text{Product mixture always formed (except with very selective bromination)}$

categories.description: Omitting termination steps when writing complete mechanisms
categories.detailed_mechanism: Students often write only initiation and propagation steps, forgetting that termination steps are essential to complete the mechanism. Termination explains why the chain eventually stops and accounts for minor byproducts.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: Writing only initiation and propagation. CORRECT: Must include all three stages: initiation (radical formation), propagation (chain reaction), AND termination (radical combination). Termination steps: $\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{Cl}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2$, $\text{R}^\bullet + \text{Cl}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{R}-\text{Cl}$, $\text{R}^\bullet + \text{R}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{R}-\text{R}$.

categories.type: Mistake: Confusing Chlorination and Bromination Selectivity
categories.description: Assuming chlorination and bromination have similar selectivity
categories.detailed_mechanism: Chlorination is much less selective than bromination due to differences in radical reactivity. Students often apply bromination selectivity (highly selective for most substituted position) to chlorination, leading to incorrect product predictions.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 'Chlorination gives 97% secondary product like bromination.' CORRECT: Chlorination gives ~57% secondary product; bromination gives ~97% secondary product. Bromination is 24× more selective than chlorination.

categories.detailed_mechanism: Free radical halogenation often produces mixtures of mono-, di-, tri-, and tetra-substituted products because the product can undergo further halogenation. Students often predict only mono-substituted products without considering multiple substitution.

examples.explanation: INCORRECT: 'Methane + $\text{Cl}_2$ gives only $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$.' CORRECT: Methane + excess $\text{Cl}_2$ gives mixture of $\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}$, $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$, $\text{CHCl}_3$, and $\text{CCl}_4$. Use excess methane to favor mono-substitution.
examples.equation: $\text{Multiple substitution occurs unless excess alkane is used}$

sections.introduction: Apply your understanding of free radical halogenation to these practice problems. Work through mechanism writing, product prediction, and selectivity calculations to build proficiency.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Major Product for 2-Methylpropane Bromination
worked_examples.problem: Predict the major product when 2-methylpropane (isobutane) reacts with $\text{Br}_2$ under UV light. Explain your reasoning.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the types of hydrogens in 2-methylpropane.
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Structure: } (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CH} \quad \text{Primary H: 9 (three CH}_3 \text{ groups)} \quad \text{Tertiary H: 1 (one CH group)}$
$\text{Bromination selectivity: tertiary : secondary : primary} \approx 1600:80:1 \quad \text{Tertiary H is 1600× more reactive than primary}$

worked_examples.final_answer: Major product: 2-bromo-2-methylpropane (tert-butyl bromide), >99% yield. Bromination is highly selective for the tertiary position due to the much greater stability of the tertiary radical intermediate. Despite having only 1 tertiary hydrogen vs. 9 primary hydrogens, the 1600:1 reactivity ratio overwhelmingly favors tertiary substitution.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bromination selectivity: product distribution depends on (number of H) × (relative reactivity). Tertiary hydrogens are vastly preferred in bromination due to radical stability and late transition state.

worked_examples.title: Writing Mechanism for Ethane Chlorination
worked_examples.problem: Write the complete free radical mechanism (all steps) for the monochlorination of ethane to form chloroethane.
worked_examples.final_answer: Complete mechanism: Initiation ($\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Cl}^\bullet$), Propagation 1 ($\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{C}_2\text{H}_6 \rightarrow \text{HCl} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5^\bullet$), Propagation 2 ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5^\bullet + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}^\bullet$), Termination (three possible radical combinations). Note: butane byproduct forms from ethyl radical dimerization in termination.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Complete free radical mechanism requires all three stages. Termination steps produce byproducts (alkane dimers) that are often minor but mechanistically important.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Product Ratio for Butane Chlorination
worked_examples.problem: Butane has 6 primary hydrogens (at C1 and C4) and 4 secondary hydrogens (at C2 and C3). Given that secondary hydrogens are 4× more reactive than primary at $25^\circ\text{C}$, calculate the expected ratio of 2-chlorobutane to 1-chlorobutane.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the number of each type of hydrogen.

worked_examples.final_answer: Expected product ratio: 73% 2-chlorobutane (secondary) : 27% 1-chlorobutane (primary), or approximately 2.7:1. The secondary product predominates despite having fewer secondary hydrogens (4 vs. 6) because secondary hydrogens are 4× more reactive.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Product ratio calculation: multiply number of hydrogens by relative reactivity, then calculate percentages. Secondary radicals are more stable, leading to preferential formation of secondary products.

sections.section_title: Summary Checklist for Alkane Substitution Reactions
sections.introduction: A quick-reference checklist consolidates the key concepts for understanding and predicting free radical halogenation of alkanes.

components.key_feature: Requires energy input (heat or light) to initiate; does not occur in dark at room temperature

components.composition: Initiation (radical formation), Propagation (chain reaction), Termination (radical combination)
components.key_feature: Initiation requires energy; propagation is self-sustaining; termination stops chain

components.composition: Consider number of hydrogens AND relative reactivity ($3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ$)
components.key_feature: Product $\propto$ (number of H) × (relative reactivity); bromination more selective than chlorination

components.function: Explains product mixtures in industrial processes
components.key_feature: Use excess alkane to favor mono-substitution; separation required for pure products

components.composition: Chlorination: low selectivity (4:1 $2^\circ:1^\circ$); Bromination: high selectivity (1600:1 $3^\circ:1^\circ$)
components.function: Chooses appropriate halogen for synthetic goals
components.key_feature: Use bromination for selective functionalization; chlorination for industrial mixtures

summary: Substitution reactions of alkanes occur through free radical halogenation, a chain reaction mechanism with three stages: initiation (radical formation by heat/UV), propagation (self-sustaining chain), and termination (radical combination). The reaction replaces alkane hydrogens with halogen atoms ($\text{Cl}$ or $\text{Br}$), producing haloalkanes and hydrogen halides. Key principles: (1) Radical stability determines selectivity: tertiary ($3^\circ$) > secondary ($2^\circ$) > primary ($1^\circ$) due to hyperconjugation and inductive effects; (2) Bromination is much more selective than chlorination (1600:1 vs. 4:1 for $3^\circ:1^\circ$) due to reactivity-selectivity principle and Hammond postulate; (3) Product distribution depends on both number of hydrogens AND relative reactivity; (4) Multiple substitution is common, minimized by excess alkane. Mechanism details: initiation requires energy to break $\text{X}-\text{X}$ bond; propagation step 1 (H abstraction) is rate-determining and selectivity-determining; propagation step 2 forms product and regenerates radical; termination removes radicals from system. Energy considerations: overall reaction is exothermic; chlorination propagation is exothermic (early TS, low selectivity); bromination propagation is endothermic (late TS, high selectivity). Industrial applications include chlorinated solvents, refrigerants, and polymer precursors, though environmental concerns (toxicity, ozone depletion) have restricted some uses. Common mistakes: confusing homolytic vs. heterolytic cleavage, assuming 100% selectivity, omitting termination steps, confusing chlorination/bromination selectivity, and ignoring multiple substitution. Mastery requires practice writing complete mechanisms, calculating product distributions, and predicting major products based on radical stability and halogen type.
